content.text: paired with a float similarity score.

from llama_index.core.schema import NodeWithScore, TextNode

retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=5)
results: list[NodeWithScore] = retriever.retrieve("quarterly revenue breakdown")

for r in results:
    print(f"Score: {r.score:.4f}")
    print(f"Source: {r.node.metadata.get('source')}")
    print(f"Text: {r.node.text[:100]}")
Retrievers are the unit you tune to improve RAG quality. Start by evaluating retrieval (hit rate, MRR) before tuning generation.
. Embeds the query, runs ANN over the stored node vectors, returns the top-k.

# Basic: top-5 by cosine similarity
retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=5)

# With metadata filter: only finance department docs
from llama_index.core.vector_stores import MetadataFilters, MetadataFilter

retriever = index.as_retriever(
    similarity_top_k=10,
    filters=MetadataFilters(filters=[
        MetadataFilter(key="department", value="finance"),
    ]),
)
nodes = retriever.retrieve("margin expansion drivers")

BM25 (Best Match 25) is a term-frequency scoring function that ranks nodes by keyword overlap with the query. It requires no embedding model and is fast and free at query time. Use it when exact keyword match matters more than semantic similarity, or as one leg of a hybrid retrieval pipeline.

# pip install llama-index-retrievers-bm25 rank-bm25
from llama_index.retrievers.bm25 import BM25Retriever

# Build from existing nodes (no embedding needed)
nodes = index.docstore.docs.values()  # TextNode objects
bm25_retriever = BM25Retriever.from_defaults(
    nodes=list(nodes),
    similarity_top_k=5,
)

results = bm25_retriever.retrieve("EBITDA margin Q3 2024")
for r in results:
    print(r.score, r.node.text[:100])
runs multiple retrievers in parallel, then fuses their results using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). RRF assigns each node a score of
by default. This blends semantic and lexical signals without needing to tune weights.

# pip install llama-index-retrievers-bm25
from llama_index.core.retrievers import QueryFusionRetriever
from llama_index.retrievers.bm25 import BM25Retriever

vector_retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=10)
bm25_retriever = BM25Retriever.from_defaults(
    nodes=list(index.docstore.docs.values()),
    similarity_top_k=10,
)

hybrid_retriever = QueryFusionRetriever(
    retrievers=[vector_retriever, bm25_retriever],
    similarity_top_k=5,        # top-5 from the fused list
    num_queries=1,             # 1 = no query augmentation; use original query only
    mode="reciprocal_rerank",  # RRF fusion
    use_async=True,
    verbose=True,
)

nodes = hybrid_retriever.retrieve("revenue growth by product segment")
instructs the retriever to generate N variations of the original query with an LLM, run each through all retrievers, then fuse all results. This improves recall at the cost of N extra LLM calls.

# Generate 3 query variants (original + 2 LLM-generated)
augmenting_retriever = QueryFusionRetriever(
    retrievers=[vector_retriever, bm25_retriever],
    similarity_top_k=5,
    num_queries=3,             # 1 original + 2 LLM-generated variants
    mode="reciprocal_rerank",
)
# Internally: LLM writes 2 semantically equivalent queries;
# all 3 x 2 retrievers = 6 result sets are fused into top-5
nodes = augmenting_retriever.retrieve("show me the margin trend")

. Retrieves small leaf nodes, then checks whether a large enough fraction of a parent node's children were retrieved. If so, it swaps in the parent node, giving the LLM wider context around the retrieved content.

from llama_index.core.node_parser import HierarchicalNodeParser, get_leaf_nodes
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, StorageContext
from llama_index.core.retrievers import AutoMergingRetriever
from llama_index.core.query_engine import RetrieverQueryEngine
from llama_index.core.storage.docstore import SimpleDocumentStore

node_parser = HierarchicalNodeParser.from_defaults(
    chunk_sizes=[2048, 512, 128]
)
all_nodes = node_parser.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)
leaf_nodes = get_leaf_nodes(all_nodes)

docstore = SimpleDocumentStore()
docstore.add_documents(all_nodes)
storage_context = StorageContext.from_defaults(docstore=docstore)

index = VectorStoreIndex(leaf_nodes, storage_context=storage_context)

base_retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=6)
retriever = AutoMergingRetriever(
    base_retriever,
    storage_context,
    simple_ratio_thresh=0.5,   # merge if >= 50% of parent's children retrieved
    verbose=True,
)
query_engine = RetrieverQueryEngine.from_args(retriever)
is designed for documents that reference other nodes — think a table of contents node referencing section nodes, or a summary node referencing detail nodes. It starts from root nodes and follows
references recursively until it has gathered enough context.

from llama_index.core.retrievers import RecursiveRetriever
from llama_index.core.query_engine import RetrieverQueryEngine

# node_dict maps node_id -> TextNode for all nodes in the tree
node_dict = {n.node_id: n for n in all_nodes}

# vector_retriever searches only the summary/root nodes
root_retriever = summary_index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=3)

recursive_retriever = RecursiveRetriever(
    "vector",
    retriever_dict={"vector": root_retriever},
    node_dict=node_dict,
    verbose=True,
)
query_engine = RetrieverQueryEngine.from_args(recursive_retriever)
response = query_engine.query("Summarise the key points from section 3.")
to implement any retrieval logic: external APIs, SQL queries, keyword-only BM25 with custom tokenisation, or multi-step retrieval chains.

from llama_index.core.retrievers import BaseRetriever
from llama_index.core.schema import NodeWithScore, TextNode, QueryBundle
from typing import List

class ElasticsearchRetriever(BaseRetriever):
    """Retrieve from Elasticsearch instead of a vector store."""

    def __init__(self, es_client, index_name: str, top_k: int = 5):
        self.es = es_client
        self.index_name = index_name
        self.top_k = top_k
        super().__init__()

    def _retrieve(self, query_bundle: QueryBundle) -> List[NodeWithScore]:
        query_str = query_bundle.query_str
        resp = self.es.search(
            index=self.index_name,
            body={
                "query": {"match": {"content": query_str}},
                "size": self.top_k,
            }
        )
        nodes = []
        for hit in resp["hits"]["hits"]:
            node = TextNode(
                text=hit["_source"]["content"],
                metadata={"source": hit["_source"].get("filename", "")},
                id_=hit["_id"],
            )
            nodes.append(NodeWithScore(node=node, score=hit["_score"]))
        return nodes

retriever = ElasticsearchRetriever(es_client, "my_index", top_k=5)
nodes = retriever.retrieve("operational KPIs Q3")
Before plugging a retriever into a query engine, measure its recall.
takes an evaluation dataset (question + ground-truth node ID pairs) and computes Hit Rate and MRR.

import asyncio
from llama_index.core.evaluation import (
    RetrieverEvaluator,
    generate_question_context_pairs,
)

# Step 1: generate eval dataset from existing nodes
nodes_for_eval = list(index.docstore.docs.values())
qa_dataset = await generate_question_context_pairs(
    nodes=nodes_for_eval[:50],   # 50 nodes -> 100 questions (2 per node)
    num_questions_per_chunk=2,
)

# Step 2: evaluate
retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=5)
evaluator = RetrieverEvaluator.from_metric_names(
    ["mrr", "hit_rate"],
    retriever=retriever,
)
results = await evaluator.aevaluate_dataset(qa_dataset)
print(f"Hit rate @5: {results.metric_vals_dict['hit_rate']:.3f}")
print(f"MRR @5: {results.metric_vals_dict['mrr']:.3f}")
Exact term match, zero embedding cost
) entry points. In production apps where retrieval is on the critical path, using
with asyncio allows concurrent retrieval from multiple retrievers or multiple indexes without blocking.

import asyncio
from llama_index.core.retrievers import BaseRetriever

async def concurrent_retrieve(
    retrievers: list[BaseRetriever],
    query: str,
):
    """Run N retrievers concurrently; merge results by score."""
    tasks = [r.aretrieve(query) for r in retrievers]
    all_results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

    seen = set()
    merged = []
    for result_set in all_results:
        for node in result_set:
            if node.node.node_id not in seen:
                seen.add(node.node.node_id)
                merged.append(node)

    return sorted(merged, key=lambda n: n.score or 0, reverse=True)

vector_ret = index_a.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=8)
bm25_ret   = BM25Retriever.from_defaults(nodes=nodes, similarity_top_k=8)

top_nodes = asyncio.run(concurrent_retrieve(
    [vector_ret, bm25_ret],
    "EBITDA margin Q3 2024",
))
When you need to retrieve for a batch of queries — for example, during offline evaluation or pre-computation — run all queries concurrently to maximise throughput.

import asyncio

async def batch_retrieve(retriever, queries: list[str]):
    """Retrieve for all queries concurrently."""
    tasks = [retriever.aretrieve(q) for q in queries]
    return await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

queries = [
    "Q1 revenue",
    "Q2 revenue",
    "Q3 revenue",
    "annual guidance",
]

retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=5)
all_results = asyncio.run(batch_retrieve(retriever, queries))

for q, nodes in zip(queries, all_results):
    print(f"Query: {q}")
    for n in nodes:
        print(f"  {n.score:.3f} — {n.node.text[:80]}")

A retrieval quality pattern that is independent of the retriever type: index small per-sentence nodes for high-precision ANN matching, but expand each retrieved sentence to its surrounding window before passing it to the LLM. This gives the LLM enough context to answer accurately without sacrificing retrieval precision.

from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceWindowNodeParser
from llama_index.core.postprocessor import MetadataReplacementPostProcessor
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex

# Parse: each sentence becomes a node; surrounding 3 sentences stored in metadata
node_parser = SentenceWindowNodeParser.from_defaults(
    window_size=3,
    window_metadata_key="window",
    original_text_metadata_key="original_text",
)

index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    transformations=[node_parser],
)

# Retrieve: ANN matches individual sentences (high precision)
retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=10)
raw_nodes = retriever.retrieve("gross margin drivers")

# At generation time: swap sentences back out for their windows
# Use MetadataReplacementPostProcessor in the query engine instead:
from llama_index.core.query_engine import RetrieverQueryEngine

query_engine = RetrieverQueryEngine.from_args(
    retriever,
    node_postprocessors=[
        MetadataReplacementPostProcessor(target_metadata_key="window")
    ],
)
response = query_engine.query("What drove the gross margin improvement?")
Complex retrieval pipelines stack multiple steps: retrieve broadly, filter by score or keyword, then re-rank. The order matters: apply cheap filters before expensive re-ranking.

from llama_index.core.query_engine import RetrieverQueryEngine
from llama_index.core.postprocessor import (
    SimilarityPostprocessor,
    KeywordNodePostprocessor,
)
from llama_index.postprocessor.cohere_rerank import CohereRerank

retriever = index.as_retriever(similarity_top_k=20)

score_filter  = SimilarityPostprocessor(similarity_cutoff=0.60)
keyword_filter = KeywordNodePostprocessor(
    required_keywords=["revenue", "margin", "earnings"],
)
reranker = CohereRerank(api_key="your-key", top_n=5)

# Postprocessors execute in order left-to-right
query_engine = RetrieverQueryEngine.from_args(
    retriever,
    node_postprocessors=[score_filter, keyword_filter, reranker],
)

response = query_engine.query("What margin improvements were reported?")
print(f"Final source count: {len(response.source_nodes)}")

Retriever filtering on metadata is evaluated by the vector store before the ANN search, making it significantly faster than post-filtering. Use it to scope retrieval to a single department, date range, document type, or any structured attribute you indexed on ingestion.

from llama_index.core.vector_stores import (
    MetadataFilters,
    MetadataFilter,
    FilterCondition,
    FilterOperator,
)

# AND condition: finance department AND year >= 2024
filters = MetadataFilters(
    filters=[
        MetadataFilter(key="department", value="finance"),
        MetadataFilter(
            key="fiscal_year",
            value=2024,
            operator=FilterOperator.GTE,
        ),
    ],
    condition=FilterCondition.AND,   # default
)

retriever = index.as_retriever(
    similarity_top_k=5,
    filters=filters,
)
nodes = retriever.retrieve("revenue growth drivers")
for n in nodes:
    print(n.score, n.node.metadata["department"], n.node.text[:80])
In agentic or multi-tenant systems, build filters programmatically at query time from user-supplied parameters or LLM-extracted entities.
Before moving to generation tuning, validate and improve retrieval in this order:
Measure hit rate and MRR at top-5
Try 256, 512, 1024 token chunks; pick highest MRR
Compare text-embedding-3-small vs text-embedding-3-large vs a fine-tuned model
Increase top-k until hit rate plateaus; note cost
Add BM25 leg via QueryFusionRetriever; usually +5–10 pp hit rate
Add cross-encoder; often +10 pp precision at same top-k
Add document-level filters if the corpus has clear sub-domains
Retrieval is a precision-recall trade-off. Increase

to improve recall at the cost of passing more irrelevant context to the LLM. Add a re-ranker to restore precision. Measure both hit rate and MRR before and after each change; gut-feel tuning without metrics rarely improves RAG quality.
each, fuse to top-5. Evaluate with
